title: Chicken Adobo
description: A true Filipino comfort classic with soy, vinegar, garlic, and peppercorns simmered until the chicken turns tender and flavorful.
imageAlt: Braised chicken in dark savory sauce with rice
ingredients: 1 kg chicken, cut into serving pieces
steps: Combine chicken, soy sauce, and half the vinegar in a pot. Marinate 20 minutes.
Add garlic, peppercorns, bay leaves, and enough water to almost cover. Bring to a boil.
Simmer covered for 30 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Add remaining vinegar and sugar; simmer uncovered 10–15 minutes to reduce sauce.
Serve over rice with plenty of sauce.

title: Sinigang na Baboy
description: A warm tamarind-based pork soup with vegetables that tastes bright, comforting, and familiar.
imageAlt: Sour soup with vegetables in a bowl
ingredients: 800 g pork ribs or belly, cut into chunks
steps: Boil pork in water until tender, about 45 minutes, skimming foam.
Add onion and tomatoes; simmer until softened.
Stir in sinigang mix; adjust sourness with water or more mix.
Add eggplant; cook until almost tender, then add greens and chilies.
Season with fish sauce. Serve hot with rice.

title: Pancit Canton
description: Stir-fried wheat noodles packed with vegetables and protein, great for both celebrations and quick family meals.
steps: Blanch or soak noodles per package until pliable; drain.
Stir-fry garlic and onion until fragrant. Add chicken, then shrimp.
Toss in vegetables; cook until crisp-tender.
Add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; toss on high heat.
Finish with calamansi. Serve immediately.

title: Lumpiang Shanghai
description: Crispy meat-filled spring rolls that are always a hit, so making extra is usually a good idea.
imageAlt: Golden fried spring rolls on a plate
steps: Mix pork, carrot, onions, garlic, egg, and soy sauce.
Wrap 1 tbsp filling per wrapper; seal with water.
Fry in 350°F oil until deep golden and crisp.
Drain on a rack. Serve with sweet chili sauce.

title: Kare-Kare
description: Oxtail and tripe in rich peanut sauce served with bagoong on the side, a festive dish with deep, layered flavor.
imageAlt: Stew with thick peanut-colored sauce and vegetables
steps: Simmer oxtail until very tender, 2–3 hours (pressure cooker faster).
Toast rice flour lightly; whisk with peanut butter and annatto into broth.
Simmer sauce until thick; season carefully (bagoong will add salt).
Blanch or simmer vegetables separately until tender.
Arrange meat and vegetables; pour sauce. Serve with bagoong and rice.

title: Halo-Halo
description: A refreshing shaved ice dessert loaded with sweet beans, jellies, leche flan, and ube in every spoonful.
imageAlt: Layered shaved ice dessert in a tall glass
steps: Layer sweet ingredients in a tall glass.
Fill with shaved ice; press lightly.
Pour evaporated milk over the top.
Top with leche flan and ube.
Mix well before eating; halo-halo means "mix-mix."

title: Tinolang Manok
description: A light ginger chicken soup with green papaya and chili leaves that feels simple, soothing, and nourishing.
imageAlt: Clear ginger chicken soup with green papaya
ingredients: 1 small green papaya, peeled and cubed
steps: Sauté garlic, onion, and ginger in oil until fragrant.
Add chicken; cook until lightly browned on all sides.
Pour in water; bring to a boil then reduce to a simmer.
Season with fish sauce; simmer 25 minutes until chicken is tender.
Add papaya; cook until just tender, about 8 minutes.
Stir in leaves; cook 1 minute. Serve immediately with rice.

title: Lechon Kawali
description: Pork belly that is boiled then deep-fried until the skin is crackly and the meat stays juicy, absolutely worth the effort.
imageAlt: Sliced crispy pork belly on a wooden board
ingredients: Liver sauce or vinegar-garlic dip, for serving
steps: Simmer pork belly with peppercorns, garlic, bay leaves, and salt for 1 hour until tender.
Remove and pat completely dry; refrigerate uncovered 1 hour (or overnight for best results).
Heat oil to 375°F. Carefully lower pork belly skin-side down.
Fry 15–18 minutes, turning occasionally, until skin is deep golden and blistered.
Drain on a rack; rest 5 minutes before slicing.
Serve with liver sauce or spiced vinegar.

title: Pancit Palabok
description: Rice noodles covered in smoky shrimp sauce and loaded with toppings, a true crowd-pleaser for gatherings.
imageAlt: Orange-sauced rice noodles topped with shrimp and egg
ingredients: 2 tbsp annatto powder dissolved in ½ cup water
steps: Simmer reserved shrimp shells in 3 cups water for 15 minutes; strain to make stock.
Sauté garlic in oil; whisk in flour, then add annatto water and shrimp stock.
Simmer sauce until thickened; season with fish sauce.
Soak bihon in water until pliable; drain and cook in boiling water 2–3 minutes.
Plate noodles and ladle sauce generously over the top.
Top with whole shrimp, egg slices, chicharon, and green onions. Serve with calamansi.

title: Kinilaw na Tanigue
description: A Visayan-style ceviche where fresh Spanish mackerel is cured in coconut vinegar with ginger, onion, and chili.
imageAlt: Diced white fish in coconut vinegar with sliced chili
steps: Rinse fish cubes in cold water; drain well.
Toss fish with half the vinegar and salt; let sit 2 minutes, then drain.
Add remaining vinegar, ginger, onion, and chilies.
Season with pepper; fold in coconut cream if using.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately while fish is just-cured.

title: Bulalo
description: A Batangas favorite of beef shank and bone marrow simmered low and slow for a rich broth that warms you right up.
imageAlt: Clear beef soup with bone marrow and corn
ingredients: 1.5 kg beef shank with bone marrow
Fish sauce and pepper, to taste
steps: Blanch beef shank in boiling water for 5 minutes; discard water and rinse.
Cover with fresh 2 L water; bring to a boil. Add onion and garlic.
Simmer on low, partially covered, for 2½–3 hours until beef is fork-tender.
Skim fat as needed; season broth with fish sauce and pepper.
Add corn; cook 10 minutes. Add cabbage and pechay; cook 3 minutes more.
Serve in deep bowls with rice and extra fish sauce on the side.

title: Leche Flan
description: A silky steamed caramel custard made with egg yolks and condensed milk, often considered a classic Filipino dessert centerpiece.
imageAlt: Golden caramel flan on a white plate
steps: Make caramel: heat sugar and water in a llanera (oval pan) until deep amber. Swirl to coat bottom; let harden.
Whisk egg yolks gently and avoid over-beating. Fold in condensed milk, evaporated milk, and vanilla.
Strain mixture through a fine sieve twice for an ultra-smooth custard.
Pour into prepared llaneras; cover tightly with foil.
Steam on low heat for 35–45 minutes until just set with a slight jiggle in the center.
Cool completely; refrigerate at least 2 hours. Invert onto a plate to serve.

title: Bicol Express
description: Spicy pork simmered in rich coconut milk with chilies and bagoong, delivering creamy heat that goes perfectly with rice.
imageAlt: Spicy pork in creamy coconut sauce
steps: Sauté garlic and onion until soft and fragrant.
Add pork slices and cook until lightly browned and fat renders.
Stir in bagoong, then pour in coconut milk and simmer 20 minutes.
Add green and red chilies; continue simmering until pork is tender.
Finish with coconut cream and cook until sauce thickens. Season and serve hot.

title: Chicken Inasal
description: Iloilo-style grilled chicken marinated in calamansi, vinegar, and annatto for a smoky, tangy, and juicy bite.
imageAlt: Grilled chicken quarters with charred skin
steps: Mix vinegar, calamansi, annatto, garlic, ginger, sugar, and fish sauce.
Marinate chicken at least 2 hours or overnight.
Grill over medium heat, turning often, until cooked through and lightly charred.
Baste with oil during grilling for glossy, juicy skin.
Rest briefly, then serve with rice and spiced vinegar.

title: Laing
description: Dried taro leaves slowly cooked in coconut milk with chili and shrimp paste, deeply savory and aromatic.
imageAlt: Creamy taro leaf stew with red chili slices
steps: Sauté garlic and onion; add pork if using and cook until lightly browned.
Pour in coconut milk and stir in bagoong.
Add dried taro leaves without stirring for a few minutes to soften.
Simmer gently until leaves are tender and liquid reduces.
Add coconut cream and chilies; simmer until thick and flavorful.

title: Ginataang Kalabasa at Sitaw
description: Squash and long beans braised in coconut milk with shrimp for a sweet, creamy, and comforting home-style dish.
imageAlt: Squash and long beans in coconut stew
ingredients: 200 g sitaw (long beans), cut into 2-inch pieces
steps: Sauté garlic and onion until fragrant.
Add shrimp and cook just until pink, then remove and set aside.
Pour in coconut milk and bring to a gentle simmer.
Add squash and cook until almost tender, then add long beans and chili.
Return shrimp, season with fish sauce and pepper, then simmer 2 minutes.

title: Pork Sisig
description: Crispy chopped pork tossed with onion, chili, and calamansi, served sizzling and made for sharing.
imageAlt: Chopped crispy pork sisig on a hot plate
ingredients: 700 g pork jowl or pork belly
steps: Boil pork until tender, then drain and cool.
Grill or broil pork until edges are crisp and lightly charred.
Chop finely and sauté in oil until sizzling and fragrant.
Add onion, chilies, soy sauce, and calamansi juice.
Mix in mayonnaise if desired; serve on a hot plate with egg.

title: Bibingka
description: A soft rice cake baked with coconut milk, topped with salted egg and cheese, then finished with a brush of butter.
imageAlt: Golden bibingka rice cake in banana leaf
ingredients: 2 tbsp melted butter, plus extra for brushing
steps: Preheat oven to 375°F. Line pan with banana leaf if available.
Whisk rice flour, sugar, and baking powder in a bowl.
Add coconut milk, eggs, and melted butter; whisk until smooth.
Pour batter into pan; top with salted egg slices and cheese.
Bake 25–30 minutes until set and lightly golden. Brush with butter before serving.
